claims.claim_text: The opening anecdote describes a specific Flannel migration that caused a critical batch job to hang every few minutes.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, war story paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: A platform team migrated a cluster from Flannel with `host-gw` to Flannel with `vxlan` encapsulation. Everything seemed fine -- small health-check probes succeeded, pod-to-pod pings worked, and Services resolved correctly. But every few minutes, a critical batch job would hang and eventually time out.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A common migration failure mode is that a switch from routed networking to overlay encapsulation appears healthy for small probes but causes larger application transfers to stall or time out.
claims.rationale: This is a detailed incident-style anecdote with no attributable source, so the safe fix is to keep the operational lesson and remove the fabricated specifics.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote says a junior engineer found that VXLAN added 50 bytes of overhead, reducing MTU from 1500 to 1450 and causing large payload drops after two days of debugging.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, war story paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: After two days of fruitless debugging (restarting pods, checking DNS, blaming the application), a junior engineer ran `tcpdump` on a node and noticed something peculiar: TCP SYN packets crossed nodes fine, but the large data payloads were being silently dropped. The culprit? VXLAN adds a 50-byte header, reducing the effective MTU from 1500 to 1450. The CNI was configured for MTU 1500, so any packet close to the limit was too large for the tunnel, and the `Don't Fragment` bit caused the kernel to drop it silently instead of fragmenting.
claims.suggested_rewrite: One common cause is an MTU mismatch: overlay encapsulation reduces the effective packet size, so larger payloads can be dropped when the pod MTU is left too high for the tunnel.
claims.rationale: This mixes an uncited war story with precise numeric specifics, so it should be rewritten to preserve the technical lesson without the invented narrative detail.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote says the fix was a one-line MTU change to 1450.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, war story paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: The fix was a one-line config change (`"MTU": 1450`), but finding it required understanding the actual data path -- where packets enter the kernel, how they get encapsulated, and where they exit. That is exactly what this module teaches.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The fix is to set the CNI MTU to match the encapsulation overhead and then verify the data path end to end. That is exactly what this module teaches.
claims.rationale: The exact one-line fix is part of the same unsourced anecdote and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: In Linux iptables mode, kube-proxy installs kernel packet-forwarding rules rather than handling Service data packets in userspace.
claims.rationale: The Virtual IPs and Service Proxies reference explicitly says kube-proxy uses kernel packet-processing logic such as iptables and packet forwarding rules.

claims.claim_text: The module claims that one Service with 1000 backends generates about 8000 iptables rules and that clusters around 5000 Services often move to IPVS or eBPF.
claims.anchor_text: - **A single Service with 1000 backends generates ~8000 iptables rules** in iptables mode. This is why large clusters (5000+ Services) often switch to IPVS mode or eBPF-based solutions like Cilium, which can handle hundreds of thousands of backends without linear rule scanning.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Large Services can create many iptables rules** in iptables mode. At larger scales, operators often evaluate newer datapaths such as nftables or eBPF-based implementations to reduce rule churn and lookup overhead.
claims.rationale: The exact rule counts, service-count threshold, and scale claims are too specific for the available allowlisted primary sources.

claims.claim_text: The module states that CoreDNS typically handles 10,000-50,000 QPS and that a bad ndots setting multiplies lookups by 5x.
claims.anchor_text: - **CoreDNS handles roughly 10,000-50,000 queries per second** in a typical production cluster. A single misconfigured `ndots` value can multiply that by 5x, because each lookup triggers search-domain expansion (e.g., `api.example.com` becomes 5 separate DNS queries before the final one succeeds).
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **CoreDNS can handle high query volumes, but search-domain expansion can multiply lookups**. A poorly chosen `ndots` setting can cause several DNS queries before the final absolute name is tried.
claims.rationale: The exact QPS band and 5x multiplier are not directly backed by an allowlisted primary source for this module.

claims.claim_text: The module claims that most CNI plugins enable hairpin mode by default.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 1.3 Hairpin Traffic, paragraph after diagram
claims.anchor_text: Most CNI plugins enable hairpin mode by default. If you see intermittent failures where a pod sometimes cannot reach its own Service, hairpin is the likely suspect. Check with:
claims.suggested_rewrite: If a pod cannot reach its own Service, inspect hairpin settings on the node and the relevant bridge or veth interfaces. Check with:
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes debug-service docs cover the hairpin edge case, but not the broader claim about most CNIs enabling it by default.

claims.claim_text: The CNI quick matrix states exact per-vendor data planes for Calico, Flannel, and Cilium.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2.2 CNI Quick Matrix, table row: Data plane
claims.anchor_text: | **Data plane** | iptables or eBPF | VXLAN or host-gw | eBPF |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Data plane** | Standard Linux or eBPF data planes, depending on mode | Backend-dependent overlay or routed data plane | eBPF-based dataplane |
claims.rationale: This row compresses multiple vendor-specific implementation details into exact cells that vary by mode and installation.

claims.claim_text: The CNI quick matrix states exact default routing behavior for Calico, Flannel, and Cilium.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2.2 CNI Quick Matrix, table row: Default routing
claims.anchor_text: | **Default routing** | BGP (no encap) | VXLAN overlay | eBPF direct routing |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Typical routing options** | BGP, VXLAN, or other modes depending on installation | Overlay or host-gw backends, depending on configuration | Native routing or overlay, depending on configuration |
claims.rationale: The current wording presents exact defaults that depend on deployment choices and vendor-specific docs.

claims.claim_text: The CNI quick matrix makes exact per-vendor NetworkPolicy capability claims.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2.2 CNI Quick Matrix, table row: Network Policies
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Network Policies** | Supported | Requires an additional policy-capable component | Supported, with extended L7 options in some deployments |
claims.rationale: The exact capability framing is too compressed and vendor-specific for an uncited comparison row.

claims.claim_text: The CNI quick matrix says Calico and Cilium can replace kube-proxy while Flannel cannot.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2.2 CNI Quick Matrix, table row: Can replace kube-proxy
claims.anchor_text: | **Can replace kube-proxy** | Yes (eBPF mode) | No | Yes (kube-proxy replacement) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Can replace kube-proxy** | Depends on the selected data plane | No | Supported in kube-proxy-replacement mode |
claims.rationale: These cells are real deployment-mode distinctions, but the current row is too exact without per-vendor primary sourcing.

claims.claim_text: The CNI quick matrix makes exact per-vendor MTU claims, including a specific 50-byte VXLAN reduction for Flannel.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2.2 CNI Quick Matrix, table row: MTU concern
claims.anchor_text: | **MTU concern** | None (no encap) | Yes (-50 bytes for VXLAN) | Depends on config |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **MTU concern** | Overlay modes require MTU planning | Overlay backends require MTU planning | Depends on routing and encapsulation mode |
claims.rationale: The row mixes a precise numeric claim with mode-dependent vendor behavior that is better stated more cautiously.

claims.claim_text: The kube-proxy mode table says iptables mode has O(n) rule evaluation and is fine for fewer than 5000 Services.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2.3 kube-proxy Modes, table row: iptables
claims.anchor_text: | **iptables** | DNAT rules per Service/Endpoint | O(n) rule evaluation | Default, fine for < 5000 Services |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **iptables** | Rules per Service and endpoint | Performance depends on rule-set size | Common default on Linux |
claims.rationale: The docs support per-endpoint rule growth and slower updates at scale, but not this exact complexity-and-threshold wording.

claims.claim_text: The kube-proxy mode table presents IPVS as the large-cluster choice with O(1) hash-table lookup and a 5000-Service threshold.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2.3 kube-proxy Modes, table row: IPVS
claims.anchor_text: | **IPVS** | Virtual server with real backends | O(1) lookup via hash table | Large clusters (5000+ Services) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **IPVS** | IPVS virtual servers and backends | Legacy Linux alternative | Older clusters that already depend on it |
claims.rationale: The current table is outdated because upstream now marks IPVS deprecated and recommends nftables instead.

claims.claim_text: The kube-proxy mode table says nftables is the recommended path for Kubernetes 1.31+.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2.3 kube-proxy Modes, table row: nftables
claims.anchor_text: | **nftables** | Next-gen replacement for iptables | Better than iptables | K8s 1.31+ recommended path |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **nftables** | nftables rules in the kernel | Better scalability than iptables in many cases | Modern Linux clusters that support it |
claims.rationale: Upstream docs support nftables as stable in v1.33 and preferred over IPVS, not the stronger current versioned phrasing.

claims.claim_text: The module states that `ndots:5` means any name with fewer than 5 dots is treated as relative.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 3.2 The ndots Trap, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: The `ndots:5` default in Kubernetes means any name with fewer than 5 dots is treated as a relative name. This triggers search domain expansion:
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kubernetes pods commonly inherit a DNS search list and `ndots:5`, so short external names may be expanded through the cluster search domains before the absolute name is tried:
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes DNS docs show the search list and `ndots:5`, but this sentence is better stated more conservatively in-module.

claims.claim_text: The conntrack section says a pod can be deleted and recreated with the same IP, leaving stale conntrack state that affects only specific pods.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 4.3 Conntrack: The Hidden State, paragraph after command block
claims.anchor_text: **When conntrack bites you**: If a pod is deleted and recreated with the same IP (rare but possible), conntrack may still have entries pointing to the old connection state. Symptoms include connections that hang or reset for no apparent reason, but only to specific pods.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **When conntrack bites you**: After a backend changes, stale conntrack state can cause confusing transient failures until the affected flows age out or are explicitly cleared.
claims.rationale: The stale-conntrack lesson is real, but the same-IP reuse mechanism is a precise scenario without an allowlisted primary source here.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table gives a universal VXLAN rule of physical MTU minus 50 bytes.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, table row: Ignoring MTU after switching CNI modes
claims.anchor_text: | Ignoring MTU after switching CNI modes | Large packets silently dropped | Always set MTU = physical MTU minus encap overhead (50 for VXLAN) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Ignoring MTU after switching CNI modes | Large packets silently dropped | Always account for encapsulation overhead when setting MTU |
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is sound, but the exact 50-byte rule is presented as universal without a directly matched source in the section pool.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table prescribes `ndots: 2` as the fix for pods calling external services.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, table row: Setting ndots too high
claims.anchor_text: | Setting `ndots` too high | DNS query amplification, slow lookups | Use `ndots: 2` for pods calling external services, or use trailing dots |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Setting `ndots` too high | DNS query amplification, slow lookups | Consider lowering `ndots` for pods that mostly call external names, or use trailing dots |
claims.rationale: Lowering ndots can help, but prescribing one exact value as the general fix is too absolute for a troubleshooting module.

further_reading.title: Virtual IPs and Service Proxies
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical reference for kube-proxy behavior, Service VIPs, traffic policies, and Linux proxy modes.

further_reading.title: Cluster Networking
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains the Kubernetes network model and the pod, service, and node IP responsibilities behind the cluster data path.

further_reading.title: DNS for Services and Pods
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers Service DNS records, pod resolver configuration, search domains, and the DNS behavior discussed in the module.

further_reading.title: Using Source IP
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows how source NAT and `externalTrafficPolicy` affect NodePort and LoadBalancer traffic in practice.

notes: Cold-start API was unavailable in this session, so the seed was built from direct module inspection plus upstream docs. The vendor-comparison and kube-proxy-mode tables were handled conservatively because several current cells are more precise than a single allowlisted primary source can honestly support.
